title: Egyptian Molokhia-Style Spinach Stew

ingredients.name: fresh spinach leaves (molokhia substitute), finely chopped
visual_checks: Traditional molokhia uses jute-mallow leaves (mulukhiyah); spinach gives the same texture and cooking technique
ingredients.notes: Traditional molokhia uses jute-mallow leaves (mulukhiyah); spinach gives the same texture and cooking technique

subs.name: Frozen chopped spinach (palak); thaw and squeeze dry before adding to the broth — works identically

ingredients.name: chicken (bone-in for stock, or use pre-made chicken stock)
visual_checks: Omit and use vegetable stock for a vegetarian version
ingredients.notes: Omit and use vegetable stock for a vegetarian version

subs.name: Omit the chicken and use vegetable stock for a vegetarian version; add a can of chickpeas for protein

ingredients.name: chicken stock or water
visual_checks: Use 1 stock cube dissolved in water if needed
ingredients.notes: Use 1 stock cube dissolved in water if needed

subs.name: A stock cube dissolved in hot water or the water from boiling vegetables works well

ingredients.name: coriander seeds, coarsely ground (or coriander powder)
butter or ghee for the taqliya
visual_checks: The garlic-coriander butter drizzle is called taqliya and is the signature element of molokhia
ingredients.notes: The garlic-coriander butter drizzle is called taqliya and is the signature element of molokhia

subs.name: Butter or 2 tbsp oil for a dairy-free taqliya; ghee gives the most authentic aroma

steps.text: If using bone-in chicken, place in a saucepan with 800 ml water, a pinch of salt and a little black pepper. Bring to a boil, then simmer covered for 20 minutes. Remove the chicken and strain the broth — you need 600 ml of this stock. Shred the cooked chicken from the bones and set aside. If using pre-made stock, skip this step.

Bring the 600 ml stock to a simmer in a saucepan. Add the finely chopped spinach, salt, cumin and black pepper. Stir, reduce heat to low and let the spinach cook in the simmering stock for 5 minutes — the leaves will wilt and release their colour into the broth, turning it a deep green.

While the spinach cooks, make the taqliya (garlic-coriander finish): melt ghee in a small frying pan over medium heat. Add all the minced garlic at once and stir constantly for 90 seconds until deep golden. Add ground coriander and stir for 10 seconds — the garlic should smell nutty and fragrant, not bitter.

Pour the hot taqliya into the simmering spinach stew and stir immediately — the garlic and coriander will perfume the whole pot. Add the shredded chicken if using. Squeeze in the lemon juice and taste for salt. Serve hot over plain white rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: spinach → Frozen chopped spinach (palak); thaw and squeeze dry before adding to the broth — works identically; ghee → Butter or 2 tbsp oil for a dairy-free taqliya; ghee gives the most authentic aroma; chicken → Omit the chicken and use vegetable stock for a vegetarian version; add a can of chickpeas for protein; stock → A stock cube dissolved in hot water or the water from boiling vegetables works well
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Spinach (palak) is the perfect substitute for jute-mallow leaves and gives an identical result. Ghee (clarified butter) is available in every Indian kitchen and is exactly what the taqliya calls for. The whole dish is similar to a simplified palak curry without the heavy masala base, and cooks in one saucepan in under 30 minutes.
